claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK. I'm asking about my daughter Alex - she's 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you live in the UK and Alex is 6 years old, which is under the age of 16.

Now I just need to confirm a couple more things:

**Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards her upkeep (for example, on food, clothes, or pocket money)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. And I'm not really able to contribute financially towards her upkeep at the moment - I don't pay anything regularly for her care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about your situation. I have a couple more questions to understand your circumstances fully.

**Is there anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example, the person she lives with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure if anyone else is claiming it for her. She's actually been in hospital for the past few months though - about 3 months now. But I do still spend money on her while she's there - you know, buying her clothes, toys, magazines, things like that to make her more comfortable.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that additional information. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital for such a long time.

This is helpful to know. You mentioned you do spend money on Alex while she's in hospital – that's an important detail. Just to clarify:

**Before Alex went into hospital 3 months ago, was she living with you, or was she already living with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: She was already living with someone else before she went into the hospital.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Alex was already living with someone else before going into hospital, and you mentioned earlier that you weren't contributing regularly towards her upkeep at that time, I have one more question:

**Were you receiving Child Benefit for Alex before she went into hospital, or has someone else been claiming it for her?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I wasn't receiving Child Benefit for her before she went into hospital.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all of this information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:

- Alex is 6 years old and lives in the UK
- She was living with someone else before going into hospital (not with you)
- You were not receiving Child Benefit for her before she went into hospital
- You weren't contributing regularly towards her upkeep before she went into hospital
- She has been in hospital for about 3 months now
- You do spend money on her while she's in hospital (clothes, toys, magazines)

Let me provide you with my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to be considered "responsible" for a child. This usually means either:
1. The child lives with you, **or**
2. You're paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.)

In your situation:
- Alex was living with someone else before she went into hospital, not with you
- You weren't contributing regularly towards her upkeep before she went into hospital
- You weren't already receiving Child Benefit for her

While it's lovely that you're now spending money on Alex during her hospital stay, the rules about children in hospital are designed to allow *existing* Child Benefit payments to continue – they don't establish a new claim.

**What you could do:**
- The person who Alex was living with before she went into hospital may be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for her
- If you'd like to discuss your specific circumstances further, you could contact the Child Benefit Office directly, as they can advise on individual cases

I'm sorry I couldn't give you better news. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to be 'responsible' for a child. You're usually considered responsible if either: (1) you live with the child, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them. In this case: (1) Alex does not live with you - she was living with someone else before going into hospital. (2) Before Alex went into hospital, you were not contributing regularly towards her upkeep. Although you now spend money on Alex while she's in hospital (clothes, toys, etc.), this spending began after she was already living elsewhere without you being responsible for her. The rules about children in hospital (which allow Child Benefit to continue if you regularly spend money on them) apply to situations where you were already receiving Child Benefit before the child went into hospital. Since you weren't receiving Child Benefit for Alex before her hospitalisation, and she wasn't living with you nor were you contributing to her upkeep at that time, you would not qualify to start a new claim now. Someone else who was responsible for Alex before she went into hospital may be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for her.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex. The child was not living with them and they were not contributing towards her upkeep before she went into hospital. While they now spend money on Alex during her hospital stay, this does not establish the initial responsibility required to qualify for Child Benefit. The hospital rules about continued eligibility apply to those who were already receiving Child Benefit, not to new claims.
